name: Banzai Pipeline
notes: World's most famous reef break. Left barrel breaks over extremely shallow, sharp reef. Needs WNW-NW groundswell 15+ sec period. Backdoor (right) fires on N swell. Expert only.

name: Sunset Beach
notes: Massive, powerful reef break. Multiple peaks. Best on solid N-NW groundswell 8ft+. One of the premier big wave venues on the North Shore.

name: Waimea Bay
notes: Iconic big wave bay. Doesn't break until 15-20ft face. Closes out under 12ft. Shore break is also famous. Only ridden by experts in very large N-NNW swell.

name: Haleiwa
notes: Classic right-hand reef break on the inside of Haleiwa Harbor. Works on W-NW swell. One of the North Shore Triple Crown contest sites.

name: Laniakea
notes: Gentle rolling right-hand reef, famous for sea turtle sightings. Good for intermediate surfers on smaller NW swells.

name: Rock Piles
notes: Powerful right-hand reef near Waimea Bay. Often has good surf when Pipe and Sunset are too big.

name: Ala Moana Bowls
notes: Long, hollow left-hand reef break. The South Shore's premier performance wave. Fast, draining, shallow. Works on S-SSW swell May-Sept.

name: Queen's Surf
notes: Gentle, rolling reef. Classic Waikiki longboard wave. Crowded but mellow. Good entry-level Hawaiian reef break.

name: Kaiser's
notes: Hollow, aggressive right-hand reef. Shallow and powerful. One of the more aggressive South Shore breaks.

name: Publics
notes: Long, consistent left reef break near Diamond Head. Very consistent in summer S swell season.

name: Diamond Head Lighthouse
notes: Consistent reef break near Diamond Head Lighthouse. Works on S-SSE swells.

name: Honolua Bay
notes: World-class right-hand point break. Long, perfect walls wrapping into the bay on NW swells. December-February is prime. Expert to advanced surfers only when large.

name: Jaws (Pe'ahi)
notes: World-famous big wave break. Waves regularly reach 40-60ft. Tow-in surfing only at full size. One of the most powerful waves on Earth.

name: Ho'okipa Beach
notes: World capital of windsurfing and kiteboarding. Also a powerful right-hand reef break for surfing. Very consistent on N-NE swells.

name: Lahaina Harbor
notes: Mellow reef break on Maui's west side. Works on W-WNW swells. Good beginner spot in small surf.

name: Hanalei Bay
notes: Beautiful crescent bay on Kauai's north shore. Multiple peaks ranging from beginner (near pier) to advanced (Pavilions, Pine Trees). Winter N-NW swells.

name: Tunnels (Makua)
notes: Powerful right-hand point/reef break at the end of the north shore road. Hollow and fast. Needs N-NW groundswell. Named for the tunnels in the underwater reef.

name: Pakala (Infinities)
notes: World's longest left-hand wave. A SW-facing point break that produces impossibly long lefts in the right swell. Can ride for over a mile.

name: Poipu Beach
notes: South shore Kauai, facing S-SSW. Summer swell season. Good for beginners.

name: Banyans
notes: Classic Kona A-frame reef break. One of the most consistent spots on the Big Island's west coast. Fun peaks for intermediate surfers.

name: Pine Trees (Kohanaiki)
notes: Multiple reef breaks north of Kona airport. Range from mellow to more powerful. Good learning spot and fun for all levels in smaller surf.

name: Lyman's
notes: Left-hand reef on Holualoa Bay. Fast and hollow at the right tide. One of the better performance waves on the west side.

name: Honoli'i Beach
notes: Black-sand beach break on the east coast of the Big Island near Hilo. Year-round waves from NE trade swells and occasional north swells. Consistent and well-known local spot.

name: Ocean Beach (SF)
notes: Iconic 3-mile San Francisco beach break. Powerful, often closed-out. Best in fall and winter. Sneaker sets and rip currents are dangerous. Cold water. Offshore winds are rare.

name: Fort Point
notes: Under the Golden Gate Bridge. Left-hand break, very powerful, strong currents, rocky. Experts only. Rarely rideable but spectacular when it is.

name: Mavericks
notes: California's premier big wave. Breaks 400 yards offshore. Wave heights regularly reach 25-60ft. Only ridden by elite big wave surfers. Shallow, bone-crushing, cold water.

name: Linda Mar (Pacifica)
notes: Protected beach break in Pacifica. One of the best beginner spots in the Bay Area. Sheltered bay reduces wind exposure. Very consistent.

name: Steamer Lane
notes: Santa Cruz's most famous break. Three sections: The Point, Middle Peak, The Slot. Long left-hand walls. Excellent fall-winter surf. Cold water (52-58F). Consistently good.

name: Pleasure Point
notes: Long reef stretch on east side of Santa Cruz. Multiple peaks from Sewer Peak to 38th Ave and The Hook. Best on NW groundswell. Year-round surf.

name: Cowell's Beach
notes: Gentle, rolling longboard wave in front of the wharf. Protected from wind. The beginner spot of Santa Cruz. Crowded but mellow.

name: The Hook
notes: Southern end of Pleasure Point area. Fun right-hand reef. Works well on NW swell at mid-tide.

name: Capitola
notes: Beach break near Capitola pier. Long, rolling waves good for longboarding. Breaks left off rock jetty.

name: Indicators
notes: Section of the Pleasure Point reef area. Consistent and fun on good NW swells. More serious section than nearby breaks.

name: Ano Nuevo / Gazos Creek
notes: Remote beach break between Santa Cruz and SF. Long beach with multiple peaks. Elephant seal preserve nearby. Often uncrowded. Consistent on NW swells.

name: Bodega Bay / Doran Beach
notes: Northern Sonoma County beach break. Consistent NW swells year round. Cold, often foggy. Sharky waters.

name: Rincon
notes: The 'Queen of the Coast.' World-class right-hand point break. Three sections: The Indicator, Rivermouth, The Cove. Long, perfectly peeling walls on W-WNW swell. Winter prime season.

name: C Street (Surfers Point)
notes: Long, rolling right-hand point break near the Ventura Pier. Very consistent. Multiple sections. Good for longboards and beginners in smaller surf.

name: Pitas Point
notes: Long right-hand point between Rincon and Ventura. Multiple sections. Often overlooked and less crowded than Rincon.

name: Leadbetter Beach
notes: Protected beach break near Santa Barbara Harbor. Gentle, rolling waves. Good beginner spot.

name: Hammond's Reef
notes: Reef break between Rincon and Santa Barbara. Less crowded alternative to Rincon. Good on WNW swells.

name: Malibu First Point
notes: One of the world's most iconic point breaks. Long, peeling rights. Extremely crowded on good days. SW swell is ideal; NW swell blocked by Point Dume.

name: Zuma Beach
notes: Wide beach break, more exposed than Malibu. Catches W and NW swells well. More powerful than First Point.

name: El Porto
notes: Most consistent South Bay beach break. Steep sand bars create powerful, hollow waves at low tide. North of the Palos Verdes swell shadow.

name: Manhattan Beach Pier
notes: Classic South Bay beach break. Fun peaks on both sides of the pier. Palos Verdes reduces NW swell.

name: Venice Beach
notes: Protected beach break. Breakwater blocks swell, Palos Verdes blocks NW. Needs clean SW or SSW swell. Mellow, good for beginners.

name: County Line
notes: Beach and point break on the LA/Ventura county line. More exposed than Malibu. Fun longboard waves and punchy beach break peaks. Parking lot at PCH.

name: Sunset Point
notes: Heavy winter point break. Reef focuses WNW-NW swell energy. Waves jack up hard. Advanced surfers only on good days.

name: Huntington Beach Pier
notes: Surf City USA, home of the US Open of Surfing. Fun beach break on both sides of the pier. Very consistent. Can get crowded.

name: Lower Trestles
notes: World Tour contest site. High-performance cobblestone reef. Long peeling rights and lefts. Very consistent year-round. Hike-in only - no road access. Tow-away zone keeps crowds manageable.

name: Upper Trestles
notes: North of Lowers. More forgiving cobblestone reef. Faster, punchier waves. Good alternative when Lowers is too crowded.

name: Salt Creek
notes: Consistent reef and beach break near Ritz Carlton. Upper (reef) and lower (beach) sections. Multiple peaks.

name: Newport Beach / 56th St.
notes: Beach breaks throughout Newport Beach. Jetty spots at the Wedge (body surf/wedge) and elsewhere. Very consistent SW swells.

name: The Wedge
notes: Famous body surfing and body boarding spot. S swell reflects off the rock jetty creating massive wedge-shaped shore dump. 20-30ft waves possible. Extremely dangerous.

name: Doheny State Beach
notes: Very gentle, protected beach break. Ideal for beginners and longboarders. Inside Dana Point Harbor provides significant swell reduction.

name: Swami's
notes: Classic San Diego reef break. Right-hand peaks. One of the best reef breaks in San Diego County. Works best on WNW-NW groundswell.

name: Cardiff Reef
notes: Mellow reef break. Consistent and user-friendly. Works well on summer S-SW swells. Good for intermediate surfers.

name: Black's Beach
notes: Powerful beach break under the Torrey Pines cliffs. Hike-in access, clothing-optional beach. Submarine canyon focuses and amplifies swell. Often one of the biggest waves in SD county.

name: Windansea
notes: Classic La Jolla reef break. Offshore reef creates powerful peaks. Rock formations focus energy. Can be hollow and fast. One of San Diego's most famous breaks.

name: Ocean Beach Pier
notes: Beach break at the OB Pier. Consistent year-round. Fun peaks both sides of the pier. More forgiving than nearby Sunset Cliffs.

name: Sunset Cliffs
notes: Multiple reef and rock bottom breaks along dramatic cliffs. Powerful, heavy waves. Rocky access points. Experts preferred. Tube rides possible.

name: Mission Beach
notes: Wide, sandy beach. Fun, mellow beach break. Good for beginners and longboarders. Very consistent in summer on S-SW swells.

name: Imperial Beach
notes: Southernmost beach in the continental US. Consistent beach break. Annual surf contest venue. Occasional water quality issues from Tijuana River.

name: Seaside Cove / Reef
notes: Consistent Oregon beach break and reef. The Cove at the north end of town focuses swell well. Best in fall. Cold water year-round (48-55F). Wetsuits required.

name: Cannon Beach / Tolovana
notes: Iconic beach with Haystack Rock. Beach break with sandbar peaks. Best on SW swell with offshore E wind. 9 miles of sand from Ecola Creek to Falcon Cove.

name: Short Sands Beach (Oswald West)
notes: Beautiful cove beach inside Oswald West State Park. Natural protection from prevailing winds. Consistent beach break. Hike-in access (~0.5 miles). Good for all levels.

name: Cape Kiwanda / Pacific City
notes: Beach break by Cape Kiwanda sandstone headland. Dory boats launch through the surf. Good range of conditions from beginner to advanced depending on sandbar.

name: Nelscott Reef
notes: Oregon's premier big wave break. Offshore reef breaks at 20-50ft. A-frame with lefts and rights. Only ridden by experts in very large W swells. Annual big wave contest site.

name: Otter Rock
notes: Protected beach break just south of Lincoln City. Cape Foulweather provides some wind protection. Consistent NW swells.

name: Winchester Bay / Umpqua
notes: Beach breaks north and south of the Umpqua River jetty. Consistent Oregon coast swell. Often less crowded than Seaside spots.

name: Westport
notes: Washington's surf capital. Three main spots: The Jetty, The Groins, The Cove. Consistent NW swells year-round. Best in fall-winter. Cold (48-52F), powerful surf.

name: La Push
notes: Remote Olympic Peninsula beach break. Powerful and cold. Sea stacks and old-growth forest backdrop. Very raw and wild. Best summer/fall when wind cooperates.

name: Ditch Plains
notes: Montauk's most consistent and popular break. Rock reef with sand overlay. Long walls on S swells in summer; more powerful in fall hurricane season. East Coast's version of Malibu.

name: Turtles (Montauk Point)
notes: Right-hand reef near Montauk Point Lighthouse. Works on S-SSE groundswells. Gets hollow and powerful. Tow-in capable on big NE swells.

name: Rockaway Beach
notes: New York City's surf beach. Beach breaks from 68th to 111th Streets. Consistent summer S swells and fall/winter NE swells. Very accessible NYC suburb surf.

name: Long Beach
notes: Long Island's main surf town. Beach break, sand bottom. Very consistent on S swells. Huge surfing community. Multiple access points along the barrier island.

name: Narragansett Town Beach
notes: New England's most popular surf beach. Consistent beach break. Protected from wind. Good for longboards. Known as the 'surfing mecca of the Northeast.'

name: Ruggles (First Beach)
notes: Powerful, rocky reef break. Most serious wave in RI. Gets hollow and heavy on big S swells. Hurricane season produces the best surf.

name: Matunuck
notes: Three cobblestone/boulder reef peaks: Deep Hole (left), Trestles (A-frame), The Point. Can handle well-overhead swell. Great fall hurricane swell spot.

name: Marconi Beach
notes: Cape Cod outer beach. Very consistent SE-facing beach break. Catches NE-E-SE groundswells well. Less crowded than southern NE breaks. Powerful currents at times.

name: Good Harbor Beach
notes: Popular North Shore Massachusetts beach break. Best on NE windswells and groundswells. Good beginner/intermediate spot.

name: Manasquan Inlet
notes: Jetty break at the Manasquan Inlet. Powerful, right-hand wave. One of the best waves in NJ. Works on S swells and NE windswells. Consistent year-round.

name: Belmar 16th Street
notes: New Jersey's most popular surf beach. Beach break with multiple peaks along the barrier island. Very consistent. Large surfing community.

name: Bay Head
notes: Classic Jersey shore beach break. Multiple peaks along a clean stretch of barrier beach. More consistent and less crowded than northern NJ spots.

name: Sandy Hook (The Cove)
notes: Right-hand point break at the northern tip of NJ. Unique NE-facing exposure. Best on NE windswells and groundswells. Often works when other NJ spots are flat.

name: Indian River Inlet
notes: Best Delaware surf spot. Both sides of the inlet produce quality waves. North side gets powerful tube-riding conditions on larger swells. Consistent from NE to SE swells.

name: Ocean City
notes: Maryland's main surf town. Beach break concentrated around jetties. Consistent S-SE swells in summer and fall NE swells. Large surfing community.

name: Assateague Island
notes: Wild horse island. Miles of unspoiled 4WD beach breaks. Less crowded than OC. More exposed than town beaches. Great fall hurricane swell.

name: Virginia Beach
notes: Mid-Atlantic's largest surf city. Beach breaks north end (85th St area), oceanfront piers, and Croatan/Camp Pendleton area to the south.

name: Cape Hatteras Lighthouse Beach
notes: Arguably the best wave on the East Coast. Cape geometry wraps and focuses swells creating long, rifling walls. Can produce point break-like rides. Expert-level on bigger days. Unique geography makes it work on both NE and SE swells.

name: Kitty Hawk / Kill Devil Hills
notes: OBX surf. Beach breaks along the barrier island. Avalon Pier area and multiple peaks. Best on NE swells in fall-winter. Hurricane swells produce biggest surf.

name: Wrightsville Beach
notes: North Carolina's main surf town south of the OBX. Beach breaks along the barrier island. Consistent SE swells. Large local surf community.

name: Folly Beach
notes: Charleston's surf beach. Beach breaks along Folly Beach. Consistent SE swells. Near the Washout area known for better sandbars.

name: Sebastian Inlet First Peak
notes: East Coast's premier competitive surf spot. Three peaks: First Peak (most competitive), Second Peak, Third Peak. North jetty focuses NE swells into powerful wedge. Kelly Slater's home spot.

name: New Smyrna Beach / Ponce Inlet
notes: Florida's most consistent surf. South side of Ponce Inlet jetty creates powerful waves. Multiple peaks. Year-round waves. Famous for bull shark population (most shark bites in the world statistically).

name: Cocoa Beach Pier
notes: Space Coast surfing hub. Gentle, consistent waves. Home of Ron Jon Surf Shop. Good for beginners. Kelly Slater grew up surfing here.

name: Ponce Inlet Jetty
notes: North side of Ponce Inlet. Powerful jetty break. Strong currents. The inlet creates consistent, quality waves. Works well on NE groundswells and hurricane swells.

name: Fort Pierce Inlet
notes: Treasure Coast surf spot. Jetty break at Fort Pierce Inlet. Consistent NE swell exposure.

name: St. Augustine Beach
notes: North Florida beach break. Consistent NE swells September-April. Summer is generally flat. Ancient City local surf community.

name: Jacksonville Beach / Hanna Park
notes: Northeast Florida's main surf area. Multiple beach breaks along the barrier island. Hanna Park to the north is a popular camping/surf destination. Mayport Poles near the naval base.

name: South Beach / First Street Jetty
notes: Southernmost East Coast surf. Waves arrive July-November from Atlantic hurricanes and tropical storms. First Street jetty best at low-mid tide on NE swells. Warm water year-round.

name: Sebastian Street (Vero Beach)
notes: Treasure Coast beach break. Consistent and accessible. Multiple peaks along Vero Beach.

name: Tres Palmas
notes: Puerto Rico's biggest wave and Caribbean's premier big wave break. Can hold 20-30ft clean faces. Deep reef. Paddle surf only. Marine reserve protection.

name: Domes
notes: Named for a nuclear reactor dome nearby. Right-hand reef break. Consistent NW swell. One of Rincon's most popular spots with advanced surfers. Can get powerful.

name: Maria's Beach
notes: Right-hand reef break south of Rincon. Consistent NW-WNW swells. Slightly more forgiving than nearby Tres Palmas. Good from intermediate to advanced.

name: Playa Jobos
notes: Isabela's premier surf spot. Reef and sand combination. El Bajo section for beginners, reef peaks for advanced. Very consistent year-round.

name: Wilderness
notes: Powerful reef break at former Ramey Air Force Base site. Hollow and fast. Strong currents. Advanced surfers only. Very consistent Nov-March.

name: Gas Chambers
notes: Puerto Rico's heaviest shore break reef. Very hollow, powerful, shallow. Near Wilderness. Experts only on larger NW swells.

name: Crashboat Beach
notes: Popular beach with a fun wave near the old crash boat pier. Good for beginners and intermediate surfers. Protected bay.

name: Steps Beach (Little Malibu)
notes: Right-hand reef break with long peeling walls. Called Little Malibu for its gentle, Malibu-like shape. Good for intermediate surfers looking for longer rides.
